coredata.dc:title: Sustainable synthesis of multi-functional carbon dots as optical nanoprobe for selective sensing of heavy metal ions

coredata.dc:description:
Background Carbon dots (CDs) are zero-dimensional fluorescent materials that attract attention due to their unique optical properties and size. This study utilizes Coccinia grandis (ivy gourd) for synthesizing CDs, focusing on detecting Fe3+ ions due to environmental and health concerns linked to iron levels. Iron imbalances can cause health issues like anemia and Alzheimer's disease, emphasizing the need for monitoring iron levels in water.
Methods
Coccinia grandis was utilized to synthesize CDs via one-step hydrothermal carbonization. Coccinia grandis fruits were carbonized through a hydrothermal process, resulting in the production of CG-CDs that were subsequently isolated and dehydrated to obtain a powdered form suitable for further analysis and application.
Significant findings Characterization revealed CG-CDs with small (∼4 nm) and uniformly distributed particles, moderately graphitized. CG-CDs exhibited strong fluorescence without passivation agents, achieving a quantum yield of 17.5 % at 350 nm excitation. The CG-CDs were effective in sensing Fe3+ ions (limit of detection = 0.53 μM) in water, validated by a Stern-Volmer plot (R2 = 0.997). This work demonstrates the potential of eco-friendly biomass like Coccinia grandis for producing efficient fluorescent sensors, aiding in Fe3+ ions detection for environmental and health applications.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation for the synthesis of multi-functional CG-CDs via one-step hydrothermal carbonization.
(a) XRD pattern of obtained CG-CDs and (b) Raman spectrum of obtained CG-CDs.

(a) FESEM images of obtained CG-CDs. (a–g) FESEM micrographs with different magnifications; EDX mapping of (h) carbon, (i) oxygen, and (j) nitrogen elements; (k) overlapping of elemental mapping; (l) elemental composition with atomic percentages.
(a, b) TEM images of obtained CG-CDs and (c) particle size distribution bar-graph of CG-CDs.
XPS spectra and FTIR spectrum of obtained CG-CDs: (a) C 1s deconvoluted spectrum, (b) N 1s deconvoluted spectrum, (c) O 1s deconvoluted spectrum, and (d) ATR-FTIR transmittance spectrum.

Optical spectral data of obtained CG-CDs; (a) UV–vis spectrum (inset: typical photographs aqueous solution of CG-CDs exposed to 365 nm UV light (right) and white light (left)), (b) fluorescence excitation spectrum, (c) fluorescence excitation-dependent emission spectra, and (d) normalized excitation-dependent fluorescence emission spectra.

Anti-photobleaching studies of obtained CG-CDs; (a) fluorescence emission spectra of obtained CG-CDs at different irradiation times under UV light (365 nm) and (b) Typical photographs of CG-CDs dispersions under UV light at different irradiation times.

Long-term stability studies of obtained CG-CDs: (a) fluorescence emission spectra of obtained CG-CDs by varying the storage times under atmospheric conditions; and (b) Typical photographs of CG-CDs dispersions under UV light at different storage times.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of obtained CG-CDs in the presence of various metal ions with similar concentration (1000 μM) at a constant excitation (350 nm), (b) Fluorescence photographs of obtained CG-CDs aqueous solutions (blank), and CG-CDs aqueous solutions with various metal ions under the 365 nm UV lamp, (c) Fluorescence emission spectra of CG-CDs in the presence of increasing Fe3+ ion concentrations from 0 to 100 µM and (d) Stern–Volmer plot of CG-CDs with Fe3+ ion concentration extending in the range of 0–100 µM (n = 10). Various fluorescence intensity ratios F0/F of the CG-CDs in the presence of various concentrated metal ions.

Schematic illustration of the fluorescence quenching mechanism through the addition of Fe3+ ions and digital pictures of CG-CDs aqueous solution earlier and later adding Fe3+ ions exposed to 365 nm UV light illumination (right top and bottom).
Comparison of the biomass/biowaste-derived fluorometric sensors intended for the selective detection of Fe3+in the water medium.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) are zero-dimensional fluorescent materials that attract attention due to their unique optical properties and size. This study utilizes Coccinia grandis (ivy gourd) for synthesizing CDs, focusing on detecting Fe3+ ions due to environmental and health concerns linked to iron levels. Iron imbalances can cause health issues like anemia and Alzheimer's disease, emphasizing the need for monitoring iron levels in water.

Coccinia grandis was utilized to synthesize CDs via one-step hydrothermal carbonization. Coccinia grandis fruits were carbonized through a hydrothermal process, resulting in the production of CG-CDs that were subsequently isolated and dehydrated to obtain a powdered form suitable for further analysis and application.

Characterization revealed CG-CDs with small (∼4 nm) and uniformly distributed particles, moderately graphitized. CG-CDs exhibited strong fluorescence without passivation agents, achieving a quantum yield of 17.5 % at 350 nm excitation. The CG-CDs were effective in sensing Fe3+ ions (limit of detection = 0.53 μM) in water, validated by a Stern-Volmer plot (R2 = 0.997). This work demonstrates the potential of eco-friendly biomass like Coccinia grandis for producing efficient fluorescent sensors, aiding in Fe3+ ions detection for environmental and health applications.

ce:section.ce:para: A family of carbon-based nanomaterials known as carbon dots (CDs) are made up of tiny, quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles that are usually less than 10 nm [1,2]. They normally consist of carbonaceous cores with functional groups on the surface that may comprise sulfur, nitrogen, or oxygen-containing moieties [3,4]. Because of their distinctive optical, electrical, and chemical characteristics, CDs are attractive options for a number of uses, such as drug delivery [5,6], bioimaging [7,8], sensing [9–11], photocatalysis [12,13], and optoelectronic devices [2,14]. Numerous approaches, such as hydrothermal [15,16], microwave-assisted [17,18], and pyrolysis [19,20] techniques, can be used to synthesize CDs. These techniques typically include the use of easily available and inexpensive precursors, such as glucose [21], citric acid [22], or other molecules rich in carbon [23,24]. In general, carbonization, surface passivation, and functionalization are common synthesis stages that are used to customize the final CDs' attributes for certain uses [25–28]. These CDs are particularly appealing for sensing applications because of their compact size, high surface area, and surface functionalization [29]. They also have excellent water dispersibility/solubility, biocompatibility, and photoluminescence capabilities [30]. Moreover, their environmental stability, easy surface functionalization, and variable photoluminescence all add to their adaptability and potential in a variety of technological applications [31,32].

Typically, renewable biomass sources like plant extracts, agricultural waste, or bio-polymers are used to produce biomass-derived CDs [33–37]. This green strategy lessens its negative effects on the environment and lessens dependency on carbon sources derived from fossil fuels. Low-cost precursors like fruit peels, cellulose, lignin, or other agricultural leftovers can be used to synthesize biomass-derived CDs [38]. Due to the ease of the synthesis process, CDs are now more easily available for large-scale manufacturing and commercialization. Because biomass-derived CDs are commonly non-toxic and biocompatible, they are appropriate for use in biomedical applications such as drug delivery, bioimaging, and theranostics. The ability of CDs to blend seamlessly with biological systems reduces adverse impacts. The optical, electrical, and chemical properties of CDs made from biomass can be regulated according to the synthesis conditions and precursor material used [39]. Because of their tunability, researchers can adjust the size, surface chemistry, and luminescence of CDs to suit particular needs [40]. Because many CDs generated from biomass have hydrophilic functional groups on their surface, they are water soluble by nature [41]. Their solubility in water makes it easier to include them in formulations that are aqueous in nature, which in turn makes them useful for applications in biological, environmental sensing, catalysis, energy storage, and optoelectronics [42]. They are appropriate for a wide range of technological applications due to their unique combination of features. Biomass-derived CDs aid in waste reduction and the effective use of natural resources by using biomass waste as precursors. This environmentally friendly strategy supports circular economy concepts and is consistent with the ideas of green chemistry.

Occinia grandis, a tropical vine that is a member of the Cucurbitaceae family, is also referred to as the crimson gourd or ivy gourd [43]. Although it originated in Africa and Asia, it is now grown and allowed to naturally occur in many tropical and subtropical areas across the world. The edible fruits, young shoots, and leaves of the ivy gourd are prized for their usage in traditional foods and herbal remedies. Coccinia grandis, also referred to as ivy gourd or scarlet gourd, has a variable elemental composition depending on the soil type, climate, and growth techniques. However, Coccinia grandis is essentially made up of the following components: proteins, lipids, carbohydrates, and nucleic acids, which are a vital part of biomass from major plants [44]. Consequently, Coccinia grandis are rich sources of carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen. Oxygen, which is mostly present in water molecules and can also be found in carbohydrates, proteins, and fats, is an essential component of plant metabolism, respiration, and photosynthesis [45]. Nitrogen is an important component of proteins, amino acids, nucleic acids, and chlorophyll, and it is a nutrient that is necessary for plant growth [46].

Iron is a critical element present in all living organisms and biological systems, where it plays an essential role in numerous biological processes. One of its key functions is facilitating oxygen transportation and exchange, as well as participating in enzymatic reactions at the cellular level. Detecting specific transition metal ions like Fe3+ is particularly significant due to growing concerns about environmental pollution. The imbalance of iron levels in the body has been linked to various health conditions such as iron deficiency, hemochromatosis, anemia, and Alzheimer's disease [47]. Maintaining the appropriate level of iron in the body is crucial for ensuring the proper function of our physiological processes. Therefore, it is crucial to monitor and regulate iron levels, not only within the body but also in potable water and the broader water environment. Several established methods, including atomic absorption spectrometry, inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry, and electrochemistry, are commonly used to detect Fe3+ ions in aqueous solutions and environmental samples [48].

CDs with quasi-spherical structures, very close to a spherical shape, make these particles interact with light very efficiently and produce beneficial properties favoring optical applications [49]. The zero-dimensional structure makes CD interact with other species at the molecular level and exhibit excellent characteristics such as good luminescence, photostability, low cytotoxicity, water solubility, and biocompatibility [50]. Unlike conventional organic dyes, CDs are photobleaching resistant, making them suitable for long-term monitoring. Notably, CDs are less toxic to human cells when compared to quantum dots, which have adverse toxic effects due to the presence of heavy metals (such as cadmium). Researchers have explored CDs as a nanoplatform for selectively detecting heavy metal ions because of their unique surface chemistry [51]. In this scenario, it is essential to develop an economical and environmentally friendly synthesis procedure for producing CDs. Additionally, enhancing the ability to selectively interact CDs with specific metal ions for fluorescent sensor application is also very challenging. Moreover, to advance this field, there is also a need for bridging the gap between lab-scale research and practical applications that are considered essential goals.

In recent years, there has been a growing interest in utilizing fluorescent sensor technologies for metal ion detection, particularly heavy metal ions like iron [52,53]. This method has gained attention due to its simplicity, high selectivity, sensitivity, excellent spatial resolution, and potential for practical and rapid monitoring. However, conventional detection methods often involve expensive instrumentation and lengthy processing times. Therefore, there is a pressing need to develop cost-effective and straightforward detection methods that offer high sensitivity and selectivity, especially for the detection of Fe3+ ions in various settings. This advancement would significantly contribute to the efficient and timely monitoring of iron levels in water sources and environmental samples. As a remedy for the above issue, this study mainly focuses on the synthesis of Coccinia grandis-derived carbon materials for the fabrication of an efficient fluorescent sensor for the detection of Fe3+ ions.

Coccinia grandis was carbonized with the use of the hydrothermal process to produce CDs. Using a commercial mixer and grinder, dried Coccinia grandis fruits were processed into a fine powder using this method. Next, 500 mg of powdered Coccinia grandis was diluted with 50 mL of water (double-distilled). After that, the unclear solution was hydrothermally treated for 24 h in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave (100 mL) at 200 °C placed inside the oven. The autoclave was allowed to cool to room temperature after the specified duration of time. By employing a microporous mixed cellulose ester membrane filter (0.22 μm), the CDs were extracted from the reaction mixture through direct filtration. The filtered CD solution was subsequently dehydrated using a vacuum freeze drier to produce the CDs in powder form, which is known as Coccinia grandis derived CD (CG-CDs). Fig. 1 provides a schematic illustration of the CG-CD preparation procedure. The produced CG-CDs were then thoroughly characterized by utilizing a range of physicochemical analytical methods.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) is a potent technique that may be used to analyze the crystal/graphitic structure of materials. The XRD pattern obtained for CG-CDs is shown in Fig. 2 (a). In the XRD pattern, the distinctive carbon peak was found to appear at approximately (2θ = 23°). This peak is associated with one of the most prevalent forms of carbon, i.e., graphite, and its (002) reflection plane [54]. The distance in the graphite crystal lattice between neighboring layers of carbon atoms is represented by the (002) reflection plane. The broad, less strong peaks show that the CG-CDs are partially disordered in character. The graphite lattice's interlayer spacing, or d-spacing, was calculated to be 0.39 nm, which slightly fluctuates from the conventional value of 0.34 nm. This variation in the value could be due to the presence of the disorder/defects on CG-CDs, which might have arisen due to the surface functional group attached to its surface.

Fig. 2(b) displays the Raman spectra of the obtained CG-CDs. Carbon-based materials usually show multiple characteristic peaks in their Raman spectra, which reveal information about their structural characteristics. The Raman spectra of carbon materials often exhibit two significant peaks, referred to as the D and G bands, which are situated at around 1360 cm–1 and 1600 cm–1, respectively. The existence of imperfections and disorder in the carbon structure is linked to the D band. It results from the breathing modes of sp2 carbon atoms in chains or rings that have disorders or structural flaws like grain boundaries or vacancies [55]. The lower intensity of the D band indicates a lower degree of disorder or defects present in the CG-CDs. The G band is attributed to the E2g phonon mode of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms arranged in hexagonal rings [56]. It shows the presence of graphitic structures by representing the in-plane stretching motion of carbon-carbon bonds in the graphene lattice. The greater degree of crystallinity or graphitization of the carbon material may be associated with the increased intensity of the G band. The ID/IG ratio, which is the ratio of the D and G band intensities, is frequently used to assess the degree of structural disorder or graphitic quality in carbon-based materials. Here, an ordered or graphitic structure in CG-CDs is confirmed by ID/IG ratio = 0.78, a ratio of less than 1 [57]. This value suggests that the disorder in the structure can be due to the partial amorphous nature of CG-CDs that arises from the surface functional groups. This value was found to coincide nicely with the observation we got from the XRD data. Thus, the dominance of graphitic nature in CG-CDs is well established and makes them capable materials for various sensing applications.

In order to study the surface morphology and elemental composition of CG-CDs, field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) with energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy studies were performed. The FESEM images (Fig. 3 ) of the produced CG-CDs demonstrate their elemental composition, size distribution, aggregation, and surface roughness. After drying, it is discovered that the CG-CDs shown in Fig. 3(a–g) have aggregated and have formed grooves on the sample holder's surface. It's possible for CG-CDs to have surface charges, which might cause electrostatic interactions and cause the molecules to cluster or aggregate. Larger clusters or agglomerates may form as a result of this aggregation tendency, particularly if the material is dried. Weak, attractive Van der Waals forces between molecules or particles can also cause the aggregation of CG-CDs. During sample preparation, the CG-CDs may become closer to one another due to intermolecular forces as the solvent evaporates. Also, the CG-CDs may shift and accumulate in specific areas throughout the drying process due to surface tension forces, which could leave the sample holder's surface uneven or grooved. These effects of surface tension may impact the distribution and arrangement of the CDs throughout the drying process.

The elemental mapping obtained from EDX spectroscopy for CG-CDs enables the spatial distribution analysis of carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen elements within the sample, as shown in Fig. 3 (h–j). Through the use of an electron beam to scan the sample surface and identify distinctive X-ray emissions, elemental mapping (EDX mapping) produces elemental maps that show the distribution of nitrogen, oxygen, and carbon throughout the CG-CDs sample with the corresponding colors being yellow, red, and green, respectively. The overlapping of every element is found on the surface of CG-CDs to get an overall view, as depicted in Fig. 3(k). These maps make it possible to locate elemental hotspot areas with high or low concentrations and determine the relationships between various elements inside the CG-CDs. Based on the strength of distinct X-ray peaks found in the EDX spectra, the atomic percentages of each component are determined. It is evident from Fig. 3(l) that CG-CDs comprise the majority element of carbon with an atomic percentage of 79, followed by oxygen (17 %) and nitrogen (4 %). The major portion of the CG-CDs was confirmed to be carbon, which originated from the carbonization of Coccinia grandis. The remaining elements, oxygen, and nitrogen, can be assumed to be part of the functional groups attached on the surface of CG-CDs. The optical, electrical, and chemical properties of CG-CDs can be tuned by the presence of these heteroatoms, surface defects, or functional groups, making them potential candidates for metal ion sensing applications.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of CG-CDs shown in Fig. 4 (a and b) provide detailed insights into their morphology, size, and structure at the nanoscale level. The above images display aggregates and individual CG-CDs, displaying their internal structure, shape, and size distribution. With differing degrees of aggregation, CG-CDs take the form of quasi-spherical or irregularly shaped nanoparticles. Surface characteristics that can affect the characteristics and uses of the CG-CDs, such as surface roughness and the existence of functional groups or defects, can also be observed in TEM images. The particle size distribution bar graph of CG-CDs that provides quantitative data regarding the size distribution of the nanoparticles seen in the TEM images is illustrated in Fig. 4(c). The height of each bar in the graph, which is usually stated in nanometers, indicates the relative frequency or abundance of CG-CDs within a specific size range. Each bar in the graph represents a range of particle sizes. It was found that the average particle size of CG-CDs was 4 nm. The distribution graph indicates that the particle size ranges from 2 to 6 nm, which sheds light on the heterogeneity of CG-CD sizes within the sample and shows that the CG-CDs have a wide range of sizes. The fact that CG-CDs are nanosized carbon materials made up of moderately graphitized carbon with few disorders/defects on their surface well matches with the results obtained in XRD, Raman spectroscopy, FESEM, and TEM results.

Fig. 5 demonstrates the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of CG-CDs. The C 1s spectrum obtained from XPS analysis (Fig. 5(a)) provides valuable information about the chemical composition and bonding environment of carbon atoms within the CG-CDs. The peaks in the spectrum represent numerous carbon species found in the CG-CDs. These species commonly include carbon-carbon (C–C)/(C=C), carbon-hydrogen (C–H), carbon-oxygen (C–O)/(C=O)/HO–C=O, carbon-nitrogen (C–N)/(C=N), and other functional groups involving carbon. Five primary peaks at 284.7, 285.5, 286.5, 287.9, and 289.0 eV were identified in the high-resolution spectrum of C 1s. These peaks were assigned to sp2/sp3 carbon (C–C/C=C), C–N, C–O, C=O/C=N, and O=C-OH, respectively [58]. This distinctive peak at 284.4 eV is caused by the carbon atoms in CG-CDs being ordered in a hexagonal lattice with strong π bonds (sp2 hybridization). The insertion of nitrogen into the carbon structure is indicated by the presence of C–N bonds, which may also indicate the presence of functional groups such as amines or amides. Oxygen-containing functional groups such as hydroxyl (–OH) or ether (C–O–C) groups typically contribute to this peak at 286.5 eV. The peak associated with carbon atoms in carbonyl (C=O) or imine (C=N) groups is 287.9 eV. The peak representing O=C–OH bonds (289.0 eV) corresponds to carboxyl (–COOH) or ester (O=C–O) groups [58] and indicates the presence of carboxylic acid functional groups in CG-CDs. Analysis of the C 1s spectrum helps to identify the types of carbon bonds and functional groups present in the CG-CDs, providing insights into their synthesis method, surface modification, and potential applications. Fig. 5(b) shows the N 1s spectrum from the XPS analysis of CG-CDs, which provides information about the functional groups containing nitrogen that are present in the carbon matrix. Three peaks that represent distinct nitrogen species, such as C–N–C, C–N–H, and C3–N, are commonly seen in the spectrum. In the CG-CDs, nitrogen atoms integrated into conjugated systems or ring structures are responsible for the C–N–C peak detected at 399.1 eV [59]. This distinctive peak can result from the emergence of aromatic ring structures by nitrogen atoms, such as pyridine or pyrrole-like motifs. The nitrogen atoms bound to hydrogen in amine (–NH2) or amide (–CONH2) functional groups over the CG-CDs are represented by the C–N–H peak that forms at 400.0 eV [59]. These nitrogen-containing groups can influence the CG-CDs' general surface chemistry and characteristics. Nitrogen atoms in nitrile (–CN) groups integrated into the CG-CDs structure are shown by the C3–N signal at 401.0 eV [60]. The types and quantities of nitrogen functionalities in the CG-CDs can affect their electrical characteristics, chemical reactivity, and sensing capabilities, which can be understood through analysis of the N 1s spectrum. The O 1s spectrum obtained from XPS analysis (Fig. 5(c)) of CG-CDs provides information about the oxygen-containing functional groups present on the CG-CDs surface or within the carbon matrix. Usually, there are two peaks in the spectrum at 531.6 and 532.8 eV, which stand for various oxygen species such as C–O and (C–OH) hydroxyl groups /(C=O)carbonyl groups, respectively [61,62]. Identifying the types and amounts of oxygen functional groups in the CG-CDs, as well as their surface chemistry, stability, and reactivity, is possible through analysis of the O 1s spectrum. The presence of these elements, their atomic bonding, and their functional group with CG-CD well coincide with the findings of FESEM-EDAX and pave the path for its usage as fluorescent probes in metal ion sensing.

The Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum shown in Fig. 5(d) of CG-CDs provides valuable information about the functional groups and chemical composition present in the synthesized material. O–H stretching and the presence of hydroxyl (–OH) groups, usually from alcohol (R–OH) or carboxylic acid (R–COOH) functionalities, are indicated by the broad peak in this 3200–3600 cm–1 band [63]. These groups are frequently present in materials that are generated from biomass since the biomass precursor contains polysaccharides and other organic components. The stretching vibrations of C–H bonds are responsible for the peaks around 2800–3000 cm–1, which shows that the CG-CDs contain both aromatic and aliphatic hydrocarbon groups [63]. The carbonaceous structure of the biomass precursor may be the source of these groupings. Carbonyl (C=O) groups, which can originate from ketones, aldehydes, or carboxylic acids found in CG-CDs, are linked to peaks in the 1650–1700 cm–1 range [64]. These functional groups may have an impact on the CG-CDs' surface chemistry and reactivity. The presence of alcohol, ether, or ester groups in the CG-CDs is indicated by the peaks around 1000–1350 cm–1, which represent C–O stretching [64]. These groups could result from the breakdown and functionalization of biomass precursors during the synthesis process. The stretching vibrations of the carbon-nitrogen triple/single bond (C–N) in nitrile groups can be detected by a peak at approximately 1350–1400 cm–1. The peaks found between 1550 and 1600 cm–1, which represent the stretching vibrations of carbon-carbon double bonds (C=C) or aromatic ring structures (C–C), validate the C=C/C–C stretching [65]. The peak observed between 600 and 750 cm–1 is likely due to the stretching of –CH2 bonds and the bending vibrations of N–H groups within the aromatic carbon structure [65]. These characteristics imply that the CG-CDs contain conjugated carbon structures or aromatic moieties, which may be involved in their sensing abilities.

When CG-CDs are dispersed/dissolved in an aqueous solution, their UV–Vis spectrum yields important details regarding their optical characteristics, such as absorption and emission features. An approach for examining how molecules and materials absorb ultraviolet and visible light can be performed using UV–vis absorption spectroscopy. In this case, as illustrated in Fig. 6 (a), it was utilized to examine the absorption properties of CG-CDs. The absorption peak that is clearly visible at 222 nm suggests that the CG-CDs include aromatic sp2 domains that undergo a π–π* transition. Electrons in conjugated π-electron systems, as those in aromatic rings, are excited from the bonding (π) orbital to the antibonding (π*) orbital during this transition. Furthermore, shoulder bumps were seen at around 272 nm and 315 nm in the absorption spectrum [66,67]. These bumps contribute to the absorption peak centered at around 272 nm and are related to the π–π* transition of C=C bonds (aromatic sp2 domains). Furthermore, bonds comprising heteroatoms, such as C=O/C=N bonds, contribute to the absorption peak centered at about 315 nm through the n–π* transition [67]. The unique absorption peaks and shoulder bumps reveal important details about the CG-CDs' molecular structure and bonding properties, especially in relation to the existence of heteroatom-containing bonds and aromatic sp2 domains. The digital photo of the samples subjected to 365 nm UV radiation and white light is displayed in the inset of Fig. 6(a). When exposed to UV light, the color of the CG-CDs changes from brown to a brilliant cyan-blue color, which amply illustrates their fluorescence feature.

The fluorescence excitation spectra of CG-CDs are displayed in Fig. 6(b). A single peak is seen in the excitation spectra at a particular 350 nm wavelength, which corresponds to the excitation energies needed to move electrons in the CG-CDs from the ground state to higher energy levels. The wavelengths of maximal fluorescence emission seen in the emission spectrum frequently align with the excitation wavelengths corresponding to the peaks in the excitation spectrum. The excitation wavelengths that produce the most effective fluorescence emission from the CG-CDs can be found by comparing the excitation and emission spectra. Through the identification of the excitation wavelengths that result in the highest fluorescence intensity, we can improve the efficacy and sensitivity of CG-CD fluorescence-based tests. Fig. 6(c) displays the fluorescence excitation-dependent emission spectra of CG-CDs over the 300–420 nm wavelength range. These spectra offer significant information into the optical characteristics and emission behavior of CDs under different excitation wavelengths. At 416 nm, the most prominent fluorescence emission peak of CG-CDs was detected when they were stimulated at a wavelength of 350 nm. When the excitation wavelength is changed, CG-CDs' fluorescence emission spectra show variations in the emission wavelength (peak). The fluorescence emission wavelength was observed to shift from 404 nm to 486 nm as the excitation wavelength changes later from 300 to 420 nm in steps of 10 nm. Up to an excitation wavelength of 350 nm, the fluorescence emission intensity progressively increases with increasing excitation wavelength. The emission intensity eventually drops at this point. The fluorescence emission peaks exhibit a red-shift, or shift to longer wavelengths, as the excitation wavelength increases. Normalized excitation-dependent emission spectra are shown in Fig. 6(d). These spectra clearly show the red-shift effect when the excitation wavelength is changed in increments of 10 nm, from 300 to 420 nm. This red-shift phenomenon suggests that, upon activation, the CG-CD's electronic transitions and energy levels alter, affecting the wavelengths at which fluorescence is released. The efficiency with which a material may emit fluorescence upon absorbing light is measured by its quantum yield (QY). In this instance, a standard fluorescence QY measuring method was used to figure out the QY of CG-CDs. Because quinine sulfate has a well-defined fluorescence QY, it can be used to compare the QY of CG-CDs. Noteworthy, CG-CDs were found to have an estimated QY of 17.5 % at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. This number represents the efficiency of the fluorescence emission process of CG-CDs, showing that approximately 17.5 % of absorbed photons are converted to fluorescence emission. With a 17.5 % QY, CG-CDs appear to have a moderate fluorescence efficiency, which qualifies them for a range of fluorescence-based applications.

In this study, fluorescence emission spectra of CG-CDs (Fig. 7 (a)) are recorded at various irradiation times (0, 25, 50, 75, 100, and 125 min) under continuous UV light exposure (365 nm). The CG-CDs are subjected to controlled UV light conditions to assess their resistance against photobleaching, which is the phenomenon of reduced fluorescence intensity due to prolonged light exposure. We assess the photostability (anti-photobleaching property) of the CG-CDs by adjusting the irradiation timings and keeping our sights on the fluorescence emission spectra. It was fascinating to observe that, throughout a range of irradiation times, the emission spectrum's intensity dropped slightly while keeping the same emission wavelength and shape. Presumably, the presence of functional groups on CG-CDs increases their solubility and dispersibility, which lowers quenching caused by aggregation and improves their overall photostability. Stable fluorescence emission is further facilitated by a well-defined size distribution and optimized surface chemistry, which reduces the likelihood of non-radiative decay pathways. CG-CDs exhibit longer fluorescence retention than organic fluorophores due to their reduced susceptibility to oxidation and degradation. The photophysical characteristics of CG-CDs, which are formed from aromatic carbon structures, are improved by the presence of a conjugated π-electron system. This conjugation reduces energy loss and promotes effective electron delocalization, which helps to sustain fluorescence emission for prolonged periods of time. Furthermore, a digital image of CG-CD dispersions under UV light at various irradiation periods is shown in Fig. 7(b). The fact that no color changes occur at different irradiation durations supports the good anti-photobleaching ability of CG-CDs, making them a promising option for sensing applications.

The emission spectra obtained for CG-CDs, as shown in Fig. 8 (a), provide details about the fluorescence properties and stability of the CG-CDs over time. Fluorescence emission spectra of CG-CDs are acquired in this study under atmospheric conditions at various storage times (Day 0, 50, 75, and 150). The CG-CDs are stored in controlled conditions to assess their stability and fluorescence behavior over an extended period. We can measure the long-term durability of the CDs' fluorescence properties by adjusting the storage durations of CG-CDs and keeping a tab on the fluorescence emission spectra. Over time, a slight drop in the peak intensity of emission spectra with consistent emission wavelengths indicates that the CG-CDs could prove stable under atmospheric conditions for a long time. Digital photos of CG-CD dispersions under UV light at various storage durations are displayed in Fig. 8(b). The finding shows that the solution's color does not change when exposed to UV light, confirming the long-term stability of CG-CDs and their suitability for sensing applications. Heavy metals, which are susceptible to photodegradation and phototoxicity, are often absent in CG-CDs. For metal sensing applications, the biocompatibility and long-term stability of CG-CDs are enhanced by the lack of heavy metal contaminants.

Because of their selective interaction with metal ions through functional groups like carbonyl, hydroxy, carboxyl, and amino groups, CG-CDs have been investigated as fluorescent probes for detecting heavy metal ions. Since certain metals can be toxic in excess or deficiency, metal ion detection is essential for assessments of the environment and human health. The CG-CDs solution's fluorescence emission spectra, shown in Fig. 9 (a), were recorded at a constant excitation wavelength of 350 nm while different metal ions (Al3+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, and Zn2+) with comparable concentrations (1000 μM) were present. When compared to other metal ions, CG-CDs showed a considerable affinity and binding capability towards Fe3+ ions, as evidenced by their excellent selectivity and specificity for detecting these ions. Certain moieties, such as carbonyl (C=O), amino (–NH2) groups, carboxyl (–COOH), and hydroxy (–OH), exist on the surface of CG-CDs. Through coordination chemistry, these functional groups are essential for metal ion binding, generating stable complexes with metal ions such as Fe3+. The oxygen and nitrogen donor atoms found in functional groups such as amino, hydroxy, and carbonyl groups are highly favored by these Fe3+ ions. Through Lewis acid-base interactions, Fe3+ ions can coordinate with these donor atoms, forming stable complexes with the CG-CDs. On CG-CDs, some functional groups can have chelating characteristics that enable them to make many coordination bonds with a single metal ion, such as Fe3+. Chelation, therefore, contributes to the high selectivity of CG-CDs towards Fe3+ ions by improving the stability and specificity of metal ion binding. In practical applications, including environmental monitoring and biological sensing, CG-CDs are promising due to their photostability and metal ion detection capabilities. Target analytes in aqueous solutions can be detected with high sensitivity and selectivity using CG-CDs due to a specific interaction between functional groups and metal ions. Fig. S1 (a) shows the fluorescence emission normalized spectra of obtained CG-CDs in the presence of various metal ions with similar concentrations (1000 μM) at a constant excitation (350 nm). The fluorescence photos of CG-CD aqueous solutions in blank and CG-CD aqueous solutions with different metal ions under a 365 nm UV lamp are displayed in Fig. 9(b). Notably, CG-CDs combined with Fe3+ ions did not show bright fluorescence; in contrast, CG-CDs that were blank and other metal ions that were combined with CG-CDs did show fluorescence emission.

Fig. S2 shows Fluorescence photographs of obtained CG-CDs aqueous solutions with various metal ions under the illumination of daylight (normal light) and 365 nm UV light. Fe3+ ions were introduced to an aqueous solution of CG-CDs at various concentrations to find the limit of sensitivity for the Fe3+ ion detection. the fluorescence emission intensities of the CG-CDs solution were evaluated at each Fe3+ ion concentration to monitor the variations in the fluorescence response. The lowest detectable concentration (limit of sensitivity) at which an apparent change in fluorescence intensity could be seen was determined by testing increasing quantities of Fe3+ ions. The dynamic quenching process of fluorescence emission brought on by the presence of a quencher molecule or ion (Fe3+ ions) can be determined using Stern-Volmer quenching analysis. Through interactions with the fluorescent probe (CG-CDs), the quencher lowers the fluorescence intensity of the probe by a variety of methods, including complex formation, energy transfer, and electron transfer. Fig. 9(d) shows the Stern–Volmer plot of CG-CDs with Fe3+ ion concentration ranging from 0 to 100 µM (n = 10). Plotting the ratio F0/F (fluorescence intensity ratio) against the concentration of Fe3+ ions on a linear scale generates the Stern-Volmer plot. CG-CDs' fluorescence emission intensity is examined at various Fe3+ ion concentrations [68]. Because of quenching, the fluorescence intensity of CG-CDs diminishes as the concentration of Fe3+ ions rise. In a linear regression model, R2 is a measure of statistics that shows how much of the variance in the dependent variable—in this case, the fluorescence intensity ratio F0/F—is predictable based on the independent variable (the concentration of Fe3+ ions). The Stern-Volmer plot's linear regression model appears to offer a good fit to the experimental data, as indicated by R2 = 0.997. The linear conjunction defined by the Stern-Volmer equation states that variations in Fe3+ion concentration may account for approximately 99.7 % of the variance in the fluorescence intensity ratio F0/F. The model's great correlation and dependability in measuring the quenching behavior of CG-CDs in response to Fe3+ ions are indicated by the high R2 value.

Fig. S1(b) represents the relative fluorescence intensities (F/F0) of CG-CDs with various metal ions. CG-CDs exhibit stronger fluorescence when interacting with other metal ions compared to Fe3+ ions. It's possible that Fe3+ ions will quench the fluorescence of CG-CDs. This suggests that Fe3+ ions may interact with the CG-CDs in a way that lessens the intensity of their fluorescence, possibly through mechanisms of non-radiative energy transfer. The linear connection indicated by the Stern-Volmer equation provides a good description of the fluorescence quenching process, and the high value validates the validity of utilizing CG-CDs as sensitive probes for detecting Fe3+ ions. The lowest concentration of an analyte (Fe3+ ions) that can be identified with a specific degree of confidence is known as the Limit of Detection (LOD). Fe3+ions can be highly sensitively detected at quantities as low as 0.53 units, as indicated by the LOD = 0.53 μM. The threshold below which the signal from Fe3+ions cannot be consistently separated from interference or background noise is indicated by the LOD value of 0.53 μM. This lower LOD value suggests that the analytical technique for Fe3+ ion detection is more sensitive. Table 1 represents the comparison of the biomass/biowaste-derived fluorometric sensors designed for the selective detection of Fe3+ ions present in the water. From the table, it could be established that CG-CDs possess lower LOD values of Fe3+ ion detection when compared to other reports that produced CDs from various other biomass. However, CG-CDs, which originated from Coccinia grandis, stand out as excellent fluorescent probes for Fe3+ sensing.

Multiple sensing analysis of the CG-CDs sensor for Fe³⁺ ions in the presence of other common metal ions (Al³⁺, Ca²⁺, Cd²⁺, Co²⁺, Cr³⁺, Cu²⁺, Fe²⁺, Hg²⁺, Mn²⁺, Ni²⁺, Pb²⁺, and Zn²⁺) was performed. This is significant because a sensor must be able to detect Fe3+ exclusively without interference from other metals in natural environmental systems, which typically contain a variety of other metal ions. Initially, an aqueous solution containing the CG-CDs was mixed with 500 μM of Fe3+and eventually fluorescence emission spectrum was measured. After that, the fluorescence emission intensities were measured for the CG-CDs solution when Fe3+ (500 μM) and all other above-mentioned metal ions (500 μM) were present in equal amounts. The goal is to determine whether these additional ions have an impact on the fluorescence response to Fe3+. A chart was generated using the selective fluorescence response (F/F0) of CG-CDs aqueous solutions toward 500 μM of Fe3+ ions and interference of 500 μM of other common metal ions with 500 μM Fe3+ ions (Fig. S3). The outcomes demonstrated that, even in the presence of other metal ions, the CG-CDs exhibited a highly selective fluorescence response towards Fe³⁺ ions. This indicates that there was little to no interference from the other metals for the sensor to be able to identify Fe³⁺ ions. Strong coordination (or complexation) between Fe3+ ions and specific functional groups (–NH₃, –COOH, and –OH) on the surface of the CG-CDs are responsible for the high selectivity towards Fe3+ ions. These functional groups and Fe3+have strong interactions that may hinder the radiative transition process, which is when excited electrons go back to the ground state and produce fluorescence. When Fe³⁺ is present, this disturbance causes fluorescence quenching, or a drop in fluorescence intensity, which enables Fe³⁺ to be specifically detected [69].

The schematic depiction of the fluorescence quenching mechanism with the addition of a comparison of the biomass/biowaste-derived fluorometric sensors designed for the selective detection of Fe3+ ions in the water is shown in Fig. 10 . CG-CDs have functional groups on their surface, including amino (–NH2), hydroxyl (–OH), and carboxyl (–COOH). The oxygen and nitrogen donor atoms found in these functional groups on the surface of CG-CDs are highly favored by Fe3+ions. On the surface of CG-CDs, Fe3+ ions can function as electron acceptors and create coordination complexes with electron-donating groups (such as –COOH, –OH, and –NH2). Electrons from the CG-CDs are transferred to the Fe3+ ions as a result of the formation of these coordination complexes, which modify the electronic structure of CG-CDs [82]. Energy transfer actions between the excited state of CG-CDs and Fe3+ions can take place in the presence of Fe3+ions. The Fe3+ions can receive energy from the excited electrons in the CG-CDs, de-exciting the CDs and preventing fluorescence emission. Charge transfer complexes can be formed when Fe3+ ions attach to CG-CDs, and electrons are moved from CG-CDs to Fe3+ ions (3d orbital) [71]. By preventing excited electrons from recombining radiatively, this charge transfer process efficiently quenches the fluorescence property of CG-CDs. The digital fluorescence image of Fig. 10 helps to illustrate this method clearly. CG-CDs are potential fluorescent probes for metal ion sensing because of changes in fluorescence intensity following Fe3+ ion binding, which serve as a foundation for sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ ions in aqueous solutions.

The CG-CDs were synthesized using Coccinia grandis via a one-pot hydrothermal heating method, illustrating a cost-effective and environmentally friendly approach to nanoparticle fabrication. XRD and Raman spectroscopy confirmed the graphitic nature of CG-CDs. The XPS and FT-IR studies confirmed the elemental composition of CG-CDs with C, N, and O and its functional groups, such as amino, hydroxy, and carbonyl groups. These CG-CDs demonstrated outstanding water dispersibility and unique stability in aqueous environments and featured ample nitrogen and oxygen-containing functional groups on their edges and surfaces. The TEM and HRTEM analysis revealed a quasi-spherical shape with uniform size distribution ranging from 2 to 6 nm; CG-CDs with these functional groups demonstrated excitation-dependent fluorescence emissions and maintained enduring fluorescence properties, making them well-suited for sensor applications. Having 17.5 % QY, CG-CDs possess moderate fluorescence efficiency, which makes them capable of fluorescence-based applications. This study centered on assessing the fluorescent-sensing capabilities of CG-CDs towards Fe3+ via fluorescence quenching (turn-off) mechanism in the water medium. The LOD for Fe3+ions was established at 0.53 μM, underscoring the sensitivity of CG-CDs in detecting low concentrations of Fe3+ ions. A linear dynamic range of 10–100 μM with a high linear coefficient (R2 = 0.997) confirmed the reliability and accuracy of CG-CDs for quantitative Fe3+ion sensing. The detection of Fe3+ions by CG-CDs relies on the coordination chemistry between surface functional groups and Fe3+ions. This interaction leads to fluorescence quenching, which enables the sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ions in solution. The fluorescence response was measured when both Fe³⁺ and other metal ions were present. The results showed that the CG-CDs were highly specific to Fe³⁺, even with other ions present. This specificity is likely due to strong coordination between Fe³⁺ and functional groups on the CG-CDs, leading to fluorescence quenching. The successful application of CG-CDs in detecting Fe3+ions through fluorescence quenching suggests broad potential for their use in sensor technologies. CG-CDs offer a promising platform for developing cost-effective and efficient sensors for environmental monitoring, biomedical diagnostics, and other applications requiring selective and sensitive sensing of metal ions. mmc1.docx
